batch_1_encoding.name: Method of Loci
batch_1_encoding.definition: Associate items with ordered locations along a well-known spatial route, then recall by mentally walking the route.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Exploits highly developed spatial/navigational memory as a pre-built ordered scaffold. Each locus is a distinctive, low-interference retrieval cue, so recall inherits the route's order for free; imagery at each locus does the item binding.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Choose a familiar route with 10–20 visually distinct loci and fix a traversal order.
2. Convert each item into a concrete, vivid image.
3. Place each image *interacting* with its locus (not floating beside it).
4. Recall by walking the route; note dropped loci and re-encode them harder.
5. Keep dedicated palaces per list, or let a palace decay before reuse.

citations.finding: Loci instructions produced 2–3x serial recall vs. controls on word lists
Memory champions rely on loci with spatial-memory brain activation; no structural brain differences
6 weeks of loci training in novices doubled word recall and reorganized memory networks; gains held at 4 months

evidence.caveats: Evidence is strongest for ordered, discrete, imageable items. Direct evidence for conceptual/relational understanding is weak; setup and image-generation costs are nontrivial.

use_when: ordered lists
speech or talk outlines
exam fact dumps
foreign vocabulary batches
avoid_when: abstract relational concepts
skills acquired by doing
material needing fast random access
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Locus reuse causes interference (ghost images from prior lists).
- Weak images at low-salience loci silently drop out.
- Retrieval is route-serial: reaching item *k* means traversal unless loci are numbered.

composes_with.note: convert numbers to images via Major/PAO, deposit at loci
palace walk-throughs on an expanding schedule
test palace contents from memory rather than re-touring while reading notes

worked_example.text: TLS 1.3 handshake around your house: front door = **ClientHello** (a client hollering through the letterbox, waving cipher suites), hallway = **ServerHello + key_share** (butler hands over half a key), kitchen = **Certificate + CertificateVerify** (a framed diploma being notarized), living room = **Finished** (two people shaking hands in MAC raincoats), back door = application data flowing out.

batch_1_encoding.name: Link / Story Method
batch_1_encoding.definition: Chain items pairwise into interacting images or a running absurd narrative so each item cues the next.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Each association is a direct cue→target pair; the story variant adds causal glue and the imagery adds distinctiveness, converting free recall into cued serial recall with zero setup.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Convert each item to a concrete image.
2. Form an exaggerated *interaction* between item 1 and item 2; then 2 and 3; continue pairwise.
3. Story variant: weave the images into one causal narrative.
4. Recall by replaying the chain/story from the start.

citations.finding: Narrative chaining yielded ~93% vs ~13% recall across twelve 10-word lists (~7x)
Link method beat rehearsal but gave poorer positional access than loci/pegs

evidence.caveats: Almost all evidence is serial word lists; little on complex material. Chain integrity is the known weak point.

use_when: short lists (≤15 items)
one-off encodings with no prepared palace or pegs
avoid_when: long lists
random positional access
material with its own meaningful structure
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- One broken link loses the entire downstream tail.
- Similar stories across lists blur together (interference).
- Accessing item *k* requires traversal from the start.

composes_with.note: distinctive interactions strengthen individual links
loci adds robustness and random access at the cost of setup

worked_example.text: Irish state milestones: a **rising phoenix (1916)** signs a **birth cert labelled 'Free State' (1922)**; the cert unfolds into a **constitution scroll (1937)**; the scroll flings a **crown off a cliff (1949, Republic)**. Each image hands off to the next.

batch_1_encoding.name: Major System
batch_1_encoding.definition: Encode digits as consonant sounds (0=s/z, 1=t/d, 2=n, 3=m, 4=r, 5=l, 6=j/sh/ch, 7=k/g, 8=f/v, 9=p/b), inserting vowels freely to form concrete, imageable words.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Numbers are abstract and non-imageable; a fixed phonetic code maps them into the word/image system where elaborative and spatial mnemonics apply. A pre-memorized 00–99 word list ('100-peg build-out') amortizes encoding cost and chunks two digits per image.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Drill the consonant table to automaticity (sound-based, not spelling-based).
2. Build and memorize a fixed word for every pair 00–99.
3. Chunk target numbers into digit pairs; pull the peg word for each.
4. Bind the peg images together (link) or deposit along loci.
5. Decode at recall by reversing sounds → digits.

citations.finding: Phonemic recoding of digits improved digit learning and retention in trained subjects
Major-derived 2/3-digit systems documented as standard among elite digit-event competitors

evidence.caveats: Lab literature is thin and old; the overwhelming evidence is practitioner adoption by self-selected experts. Weeks of up-front cost before break-even.

use_when: long digit strings
constants, dates, ports, IDs
any recurring heavy numeric load
avoid_when: occasional short numbers (overhead exceeds benefit)
learners unwilling to invest setup time
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Slow, error-prone decoding if the consonant table isn't automatic.
- Applying the code to spelling instead of sound produces ambiguous pegs.

composes_with.note: peg images go into palaces for long numbers
PAO compresses 6 digits per locus using Major-derived persons/objects
the 00–99 lexicon itself must be drilled to automaticity and maintained

worked_example.text: e ≈ 2.718: pairs 27 / 18 → 27 = n,k = 'NecK'; 18 = d,v = 'DoVe'. Image: a dove balancing on a giraffe's neck. Decode: reversed sound-by-sound gives 2–7–1–8.

batch_1_encoding.name: PAO (Person–Action–Object)
batch_1_encoding.definition: Assign each 00–99 pair a Person performing an Action on an Object; any 6 digits compress into one composite scene (Person of pair 1 + Action of pair 2 + Object of pair 3).
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Triples the compression of a plain 2-digit peg system (6 digits per image), consuming fewer loci, and exploits how easily memory binds agent–action–patient scenes.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Build a 00–99 PAO table (often seeded from Major or Dominic codes).
2. Drill each component (P, A, O separately) to instant recall.
3. Chunk digits in sixes; compose the scene P(pair1)+A(pair2)+O(pair3).
4. Place composite scenes at loci; decode by unpacking each role.

citations.finding: PAO documented as the dominant intermediate system in competitive memory for digits and cards
Theoretical basis only: skilled memory arises from meaningful hierarchical chunking (not a PAO trial)

evidence.caveats: No controlled trials of PAO itself; justification is theoretical (chunking + imagery) plus expert practice consensus.

use_when: heavy digit/card memorization
memory sport
long constants where Major pegs consume too many loci
avoid_when: casual learners
non-numeric material
anyone unwilling to maintain a 300-component table
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Role confusion under speed (whose action was it?).
- Table decays without maintenance drills.
- Setup measured in weeks to months.

composes_with.note: one PAO scene per locus = 6 digits per locus
PAO tables are usually generated from Major phonetics
component tables need scheduled maintenance

worked_example.text: π digits 141592 → 14/15/92: Person(14)= a **Tsar**, Action(15)= **towelling off**, Object(92)= a **frying pan**. One scene — a Tsar towelling off a frying pan — stores all six digits at a single locus.

batch_1_encoding.name: Dominic System
batch_1_encoding.definition: Digit pairs map to letter pairs (1=A, 2=B, 3=C, 4=D, 5=E, 6=S, 7=G, 8=H, 9=N, 0=O) which become a memorable person's initials, each with a signature action; 4 digits = Person(pair1) + Action(pair2).
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Person-first variant of PAO: proper names and celebrity schemas are highly distinctive and pre-elaborated, lowering the image-generation cost relative to arbitrary Major words.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Learn the digit→letter code.
2. Assign a person to each 00–99 pair by initials, plus a signature action per person.
3. Encode 4-digit chunks as Person + (other person's) Action.
4. Place scenes at loci for longer numbers.

citations.finding: System used to win eight World Memory Championships; practitioner method, never lab-trialed

evidence.caveats: Essentially untested in labs; treat as a PAO-family variant whose plausibility rides on the same chunking + imagery mechanisms.

use_when: learners who find people easier to image than objects
dates and 4-digit codes
avoid_when: learners without a rich stock of vivid public/personal figures
non-numeric material
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Initial collisions force awkward person choices.
- People you feel nothing about make weak pegs.

composes_with.note: person+action scenes placed at loci
same architecture; letters instead of phonetics, P+A instead of P+A+O

worked_example.text: 1815 (Waterloo): 18 = A,H → **Audrey Hepburn**; 15 = A,E → Einstein, whose signature action is **scribbling equations**. Audrey Hepburn scribbling equations on a battlefield = 1815.

batch_1_encoding.name: Number–Shape System
batch_1_encoding.definition: Each digit 0–9 becomes an object it visually resembles (1=candle, 2=swan, 3=handcuffs, 8=snowman, 0=ring), used as pegs or to image short numbers.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Zero-training visual mapping: digits become images by resemblance rather than a learned code, giving instant (but capacity-limited) access to imagery mnemonics.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Fix one shape per digit and never change it.
2. For a short number, image the digit-shapes interacting in order.
3. As pegs: attach item *n* to shape *n* via interactive imagery.

citations.finding: Peg pretraining improved paced serial learning (rhyme pegs; shape variant assumed to generalize)

evidence.caveats: The shape variant itself is rarely tested directly; evidence is inherited from the peg literature. Hard ceiling of 10 pegs.

use_when: children or first-ever mnemonic
lists of ≤10
quick hooks for 1–3 digit numbers
avoid_when: long numbers
learners ready to invest in the Major system
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Ambiguous shape choices (is 5 a hook or a seahorse?) cause decode errors.
- Does not scale past ten positions.

composes_with.note: one of the standard beginner peg sets
interaction quality carries the effect

worked_example.text: Speed of sound ≈ 343 m/s: **handcuffs (3)** locked to the bow of a **sailboat (4)**, another pair of **handcuffs (3)** at the stern — the boat outrunning a jet's roar.

batch_1_encoding.name: Number–Rhyme System
batch_1_encoding.definition: Digits map to rhyming pegs (one–bun, two–shoe, three–tree, four–door...); items are imaged interacting with the peg at their position.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Rhyme supplies an automatic, pre-memorized ordered cue set; interactive imagery binds item→peg, enabling direct random access by position — unlike chains, a lost item doesn't break neighbors.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Memorize the rhyme pegs once (usually already known).
2. For each list position, form a vivid interaction between the peg image and the item.
3. Retrieve any position directly: say the rhyme, see the scene.

citations.finding: One-bun pegs improved serial recall given sufficient encoding time per item
Pegs ≈ loci, both > rehearsal, with pegs giving good positional access

evidence.caveats: Ceiling of ~10–20 pegs; evidence mostly concrete word lists at short retention.

use_when: short ordered lists needing positional access
ranked facts (top-N by position)
avoid_when: lists beyond the peg set
abstract items you can't image without a concretization step
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Reusing pegs across lists in the same period causes interference.
- Static (non-interacting) peg+item images fail quietly.

composes_with.note: the canonical pegword set
peg bindings decay like any association

worked_example.text: First elements by atomic number: one–bun = a **hydrogen balloon baked into a bun**; two–shoe = a **shoe floating away on helium**; three–tree = **lithium batteries growing on a tree**.

batch_1_encoding.name: Peg Systems (general)
batch_1_encoding.definition: Any pre-memorized, ordered set of concrete 'hooks' (rhymes, shapes, alphabet images, body parts, Major words) to which new items are attached by interactive imagery.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Separates the *ordering* problem (solved once by the peg list) from the *binding* problem (one vivid image per item). Pegs give O(1) positional access and are reusable scaffolds — loci are simply spatial pegs.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Acquire a peg set sized to the job (10 rhymes → 100 Major words).
2. Overlearn it to instant recall.
3. Bind each new item to its peg with a distinct interaction.
4. Retrieve by peg number; drill bindings on a schedule.

citations.finding: Pegword pretraining improved serial learning under paced presentation
Pegs and loci comparable and superior to rehearsal for ordered recall
Mnemonic gains can fade faster than rote at long delays without relearning

evidence.caveats: Most trials are short-retention word-list studies; durability requires spaced retrieval of the bindings.

use_when: ordered material with positional lookup
recurring list-memorization workflows
avoid_when: relational/conceptual structure (use the structure itself)
one-off trivial lists
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Peg reuse without decay time → cross-list interference.
- Treating pegs as storage rather than cues: the interaction image is the storage.

composes_with.note: loci = spatial pegs; choose by whether space or number is the natural index
Major/PAO are 100-peg numeric builds

worked_example.text: Body pegs for the PICERL incident-response phases: feet=**P**reparation (boots), knees=**I**dentification (kneeling to inspect a log), waist=**C**ontainment (belt cinched tight), chest=**E**radication (scrubbing), shoulders=**R**ecovery (carrying a server), head=**L**essons (lightbulb).

batch_1_encoding.name: Mind Mapping
batch_1_encoding.definition: A radial diagram: central concept branching into keyword-labeled sub-branches with color and images.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Claimed: imagery + spatial layout + color. Best-supported ingredient is the *generative act of selecting and organizing* — an organization/elaboration effect — not the radial format itself.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Put the target concept at center.
2. Add major branches as single keywords; sub-branch details.
3. Draw cross-links between branches and label *why* they connect.
4. Redraw from memory later (converts it into retrieval practice).

citations.finding: Small recall advantage over preferred study at 1 week, but lower motivation; benefit tied to actually applying the technique
Meta-analysis: node-link mapping shows moderate benefits — strongest for *concept maps* with labeled relations, not Buzan-style maps

evidence.caveats: Buzan-style radial maps specifically have weak evidence. Concept mapping — explicit labeled edges and cross-links — is the better-supported cousin, and maps directly onto a knowledge-graph substrate.

use_when: initial survey/brainstorm of a domain
planning talks or essays
externalizing structure to seed or review a KG
avoid_when: verbatim/detail retention
as a substitute for retrieval practice
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Decorative mapping: pretty branches, zero processing.
- Copying the source's structure instead of generating your own kills the organization benefit.

composes_with.note: closed-book map drawing, then compare — mapping becomes testing
branches make the learner's chunk boundaries visible and correctable

worked_example.text: Center: **OAuth 2.0**. Branches: roles, grant types, tokens, attacks. The cross-link *attacks → grant types* ('implicit flow → token leakage via redirect') is where the map earns its keep — labeled cross-links are the concept-map ingredient with actual evidence.

batch_1_encoding.name: Multi-Sensory / Elaborative Imagery
batch_1_encoding.definition: Deliberately encode material as vivid mental images that engage multiple senses and, critically, show the to-be-associated items *interacting*.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Dual coding (verbal + imaginal traces) plus relational binding: interacting images strengthen the associative link itself, whereas side-by-side images only strengthen the items. Sensory richness adds retrieval routes and distinctiveness.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Concretize each element (abstract terms first pass through a metaphor or keyword).
2. Build one scene where the elements act on each other.
3. Add 1–2 non-visual channels: sound, smell, motion, touch.
4. Replay once immediately, then drill on a schedule.

citations.finding: Imageability and imagery instructions strongly predict and improve paired-associate learning
Interacting imagery roughly doubled paired-associate recall vs. separated images

evidence.caveats: Concreteness-dependent — abstract material needs a concretization step; learners with aphantasia need verbal-elaborative substitutes.

use_when: paired associations
vocabulary and terminology
names–faces
avoid_when: purely formal manipulation skills (proofs, derivations) where practice beats picturing
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Static side-by-side images (no interaction) — the most common silent failure.
- Generic recycled images blur across items.

composes_with.note: every mnemonic above runs on this
the keyword→meaning link is an interactive image
imagery is learner-generated dual coding

worked_example.text: Meeting a 'Rosalind': rose petals wound in a double-helix lattice tumbling from her hair (Rosalind Franklin) — smell the rose, hear the petals rustle. Two senses, one interaction, name bound to face.

batch_1_encoding.name: Exaggeration / Bizarreness
batch_1_encoding.definition: Make mnemonic images absurd, exaggerated, or impossible on the theory that weird is inherently more memorable.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: The real driver is *distinctiveness at retrieval*: bizarre items win only when they stand out against a common backdrop (mixed lists); in all-bizarre lists the advantage vanishes. Exaggeration also incidentally forces interaction and specificity, which genuinely help.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Encode normally first (concrete + interacting).
2. Spend bizarreness selectively — on the items most at risk of blending in.
3. Prefer exaggerated *interaction* (size, number, violation of physics) over gross-out shock.
4. Keep a common baseline so the bizarre items have something to contrast with.

citations.finding: Bizarreness advantage in mixed lists, absent in pure lists — a distinctiveness effect, not stronger encoding
Interaction between images improved recall; bizarreness per se did not
Universal advice in memory sport — practitioner consensus overstates the lab picture

evidence.caveats: Budget it: if everything is bizarre, nothing is distinctive. Vividness and interaction matter more than weirdness.

use_when: a few high-confusion items within otherwise mundane material
avoid_when: as a blanket policy across all images
contexts where offensive imagery would distract
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Arms race of grotesquerie with diminishing returns.
- Bizarre-but-static images: the weirdness replaced the interaction instead of amplifying it.

composes_with.note: spend on the weakest links in a chain
a salience dial on top of interaction, not a substitute

worked_example.text: Encoding 'race condition': two sprinters diving through one revolving door and fusing into a corrupted blur. Silly — but the *interaction through a shared resource* is doing the mnemonic work; the absurdity just keeps it from blending into other concurrency images.

batch_1_encoding.name: Keyword Method
batch_1_encoding.definition: Learn a foreign or technical term by (1) finding a familiar word that sounds like it (the keyword) and (2) forming an image of the keyword interacting with the term's meaning.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Bridges an arbitrary sound→meaning mapping through two easy links: acoustic (term→keyword) and imaginal (keyword→meaning). Converts rote paired-associate learning into elaborated interactive imagery.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Hear/say the new term; pick a concrete keyword that sounds like (part of) it.
2. Image the keyword interacting with the meaning — not merely beside it.
3. Recall path: term → keyword → image → meaning.
4. Schedule spaced retrieval; the method's gains decay fastest without it.

citations.finding: Keyword group hit ~88% vs ~28% control on Spanish vocabulary tests
Review: robust benefits across languages, ages, and materials
Advantage shrank or reversed at long delays vs. rote in some conditions — faster forgetting without relearning

evidence.caveats: Strongest for receptive recall at short/medium delays; must be paired with spaced retrieval or gains erode. Keyword generation is hard for some words and low-imagery learners.

use_when: foreign vocabulary
jargon and taxonomy terms
drug/species/protocol names
avoid_when: productive fluency and pronunciation (needs production practice)
abstract function words with no imageable meaning
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- The keyword hijacks pronunciation of the real term.
- Retrieving the keyword but not the keyword→meaning image (the second link was under-encoded).

composes_with.note: decay is the documented failure mode; schedule reviews
the second link is an interactive image
place keyword images along a route for vocabulary sets

worked_example.text: Manx Gaelic *moddey* ('dog') sounds like 'muddy' → a muddy dog shaking itself dry over a white carpet. Recall: moddey → muddy → that dog → 'dog'.

batch_1_encoding.name: Cognitive Chunking
batch_1_encoding.definition: Recode many low-level units into fewer meaningful higher-order units, spending long-term-memory patterns to stretch effective working-memory capacity.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Working memory holds ~4 chunks *regardless of chunk size*; expertise is a library of retrievable patterns that lets large structures occupy one slot. Hierarchies of chunks give roughly logarithmic effective capacity.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Find or impose meaningful boundaries aligned with the material's real structure.
2. Name each chunk; the label becomes its retrieval handle.
3. Build upward: chunks of chunks.
4. Drill chunk recognition until it's perceptual, not deliberate.

citations.finding: Capacity is constant in chunks, not items — recoding expands span
Chess masters recall real positions via larger chunks; no advantage on random boards
Undergraduate reached 79-digit span by recoding digits as running times — skill, not raw capacity

evidence.caveats: Chunks must already exist in long-term memory to be spent; imposed boundaries that fight the material's structure actively hurt.

use_when: everything — it is the substrate of expertise
syntax patterns, idioms, board states, digit groups
avoid_when: never truly avoided; but don't impose arbitrary groupings on structured material
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Chunk boundaries misaligned with real structure (e.g., splitting an IP across octets' natural dots).
- Labels without content: naming a chunk you can't unpack.

composes_with.note: digit pegs are engineered chunks
studying examples is how solution-schema chunks form
one composite scene per locus is a chunk

worked_example.text: Reading `^(?:\d{1,3}\.){3}\d{1,3}$` as one chunk — 'naive IPv4 matcher' — instead of ~20 tokens. An expert stores the pattern and its known flaw (matches 999.999.999.999) as a single retrievable unit.

batch_1_encoding.name: Dual Coding
batch_1_encoding.definition: Present and encode information in complementary verbal and visual forms (diagram + explanation), yielding two mutually cueing memory traces.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Paivio: separate verbal and imaginal systems with referential links between them — two retrieval routes instead of one. Mayer: words + pictures beat words alone when spatially/temporally integrated, because both channels work in parallel without overload.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Pair every key verbal explanation with a matched visual (diagram, timeline, schematic).
2. Integrate them: labels on the diagram, not in a separate block.
3. Have the learner translate one code into the other (describe the diagram; sketch the text).
4. Cut decorative imagery — relevance is the filter.

citations.finding: Dual coding theory: picture superiority and concreteness effects across paradigms
Integrated narration + animation improved transfer vs. either alone or separated in time

evidence.caveats: This is not learning styles: everyone benefits from both channels. Decorative pictures and redundant on-screen text can backfire (coherence/redundancy effects).

use_when: structures, processes, architectures
anything with spatial or causal layout
avoid_when: content with no genuine visual structure — forced diagrams add noise
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Seductive details: interesting-but-irrelevant graphics depress learning.
- Split attention: diagram on one page, explanation on another.

composes_with.note: example + diagram of the example
self-built imagery when no diagram is supplied

worked_example.text: TCP three-way handshake: a sequence diagram (SYN →, ← SYN-ACK, ACK →) with the one-line verbal gloss per arrow ('client proposes; server accepts and proposes back; client confirms'). Learner later reproduces the diagram from the words, then the words from the diagram.

batch_1_encoding.name: Rhymed Formula Verse
batch_1_encoding.definition: Compress an ordered body of facts (a table, a formula set, a procedure) into a short rhymed, rhythmic verse whose cadence forces the items back out in order.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Rhythm and rhyme act as external retrieval constraints: each line's meter and end-sound narrow the candidate words, so partial recall completes itself (phonological cueing + chunking). The 九九歌 (nine-nines multiplication chant, attested ~2,200 years ago) survives precisely because a 9x9 table recited to a fixed cadence becomes a motor routine, not a lookup.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Reduce the material to the irreducible sequence (one fact per line or half-line).
2. Fix a meter first (syllable count per line), then rhyme the line-ends.
3. Say it aloud while drafting — if it doesn't chant, it won't stick.
4. Rehearse to fluency, then switch to retrieval: recite from line cues only, checking against the full list.

citations.finding: Rhyme, meter, and imagery are the organizational constraints that make oral traditions (ballads, epics) verbatim-stable across generations
Rhymed word pairs recalled better than unrhymed controls — phonological relatedness is a free cue

evidence.caveats: Millennia of classroom use (九九歌, alphabet song) is strong tradition evidence but thin controlled lab evidence. Verse composition cost is high; the rhyme can outlive the meaning (learners chant the verse without decoding it).

use_when: ordered sequences that must come out verbatim (tables, formulas, procedure steps)
material for children or low-literacy contexts
avoid_when: material too large for a verse (> ~30 items)
facts where paraphrase tolerance is zero and a misheard rhyme corrupts the fact
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Verse recalled, meaning lost: the chant decouples from the facts it encodes.
- Rhyme-first authoring distorts the fact to fit the meter.

composes_with.note: each line is a rhythm-bounded chunk
verse supplies the verbal code; pair with a scene image
recite from cues, don't re-read the verse

worked_example.text: Thirty days hath September, / April, June, and November — the rhyme and meter make the exception list self-completing; you cannot stop mid-line.

batch_1_encoding.name: Trisyllabic Chunking
batch_1_encoding.definition: Phrase an ordered list as a chain of three-beat chunks (three syllables, words, or beats per group), exploiting the near-universal preference for triple rhythm in serial recall.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Chunking compresses a list into superordinate units, and a fixed 3-beat frame makes each unit self-checking: a malformed chunk is immediately audible. The 三字经 (Three-Character Classic, ~13th c.) taught a whole curriculum in 3-character lines; the same frame appears in 'Stop, Drop, and Roll' and 'Lights, Camera, Action'.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Break the ordered material into groups of exactly three beats (3 syllables, or 3 short words).
2. Keep the item order inside the chunks; the beat, not grammar, carries the sequence.
3. Tap or speak the rhythm while rehearsing — motor entrainment deepens the trace.
4. Test by reciting chunk-boundaries only, then expanding each chunk.

citations.finding: The magical number seven, plus or minus two — chunking is the classic escape from span limits
Sequences parsed into rhythmic groups are recalled better than ungrouped controls; 3–4 item groups optimal

evidence.caveats: Group sizes of 3–4 are optimal; forcing 3-beat phrasing onto non-uniform material can pad or truncate facts. Rhythm aids order, not comprehension.

use_when: short ordered procedures and checklists (safety drills, mnemonics of steps)
lists of 6–15 items that resist meaning-based grouping
avoid_when: hierarchical or relational material (use a map or palace)
items that cannot be honestly compressed to the beat
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Beat over content: the item gets mangled to fit three beats and the fact is corrupted.
- Chunks recalled, order across chunks lost.

composes_with.note: rhythm-fixed chunking with an audible error signal
triplets plus end-rhyme = a full chant

worked_example.text: Fire-safety response as triplets: 'Stop — Drop — Roll' / 'Crawl — Low — Go'. Each three-beat chunk is self-checking; a two-beat slip is heard instantly.

batch_1_encoding.name: Number Homophone Pegs
batch_1_encoding.definition: Map numbers to words or phrases that sound like their digits read aloud (in the learner's own language/dialect), turning arbitrary figures into meaningful, imageable phrases.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Phonological recoding converts a meaningless digit string into a semantic chunk that already has hooks in long-term memory — a free, pre-memorized peg system. Chinese internet culture demonstrates it at scale: 520 (wǔ èr líng) ≈ 'I love you' (wǒ ài nǐ), 168 = 'road to prosperity', 88 = 'bye-bye'; Cantonese license-plate premiums for 8 and penalties for 4 show the mapping is automatic for a whole population.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Read the digits aloud in the learner's strongest language; listen for the nearest familiar word/phrase per digit run.
2. Chain the soundalikes into one short phrase or image, ordered to match the digits.
3. Record the digit→sound mapping once and reuse it — it becomes a personal peg system.
4. Drill both directions: number → phrase and phrase → number.

citations.finding: Transfer-appropriate processing: phonological encodings excel when the retrieval task is phonological; meaningful recodings beat rote digit rehearsal
Mnemonic value of pronounceability and meaningfulness for digit strings; recoding beats rehearsal

evidence.caveats: Language- and dialect-specific — a mapping that sings in Mandarin is noise in English (where the Major System is the established cousin). Homophone drift (loose pronunciation) corrupts digits silently.

use_when: short numbers that must be exact (dates, codes, ports, constants)
learners whose L1 has rich number homophones
avoid_when: long digit strings (use PAO/Major)
precision-critical digits where a fuzzy homophone can swap a digit
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Homophone drift: the phrase is recalled but maps back to the wrong digit.
- One-off mappings reinvented per number instead of a stable digit→sound table.

composes_with.note: phonetic digit encoding, culture-tuned instead of consonant-fixed
the homophones act as pre-existing pegs
image the homophone phrase bizarrely

worked_example.text: Remembering a 1688 hotline: 168 'road to prosperity' + 88 'bye-bye' → a golden road with a waving hand at the end. Digits come back by sounding out the scene.

batch_1_encoding.name: Hand Loci
aliases: 掐指一算 (Qiā Zhǐ Yī Suàn)
batch_1_encoding.definition: Use the ordered joints and knuckles of one hand as a fixed 12-locus spatial scaffold, placing one image per joint — a body-based memory palace for small ordered sets.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: A portable, always-available, over-learned spatial order (the finger joints), so the loci need zero setup and the body's proprioceptive trace adds a motor cue to the spatial one. 掐指一算 ('pinching the fingers to reckon') — the folk method of cycling calendrical cycles across the 12 finger joints — is the classical proof the scaffold works.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Fix the canonical map ONCE: thumb tip touches the index finger's three joints in order, then middle, ring, little — 12 loci, always traversed the same direction.
2. Convert each item to a concrete image and place it ON the joint (pinching it as you encode).
3. Recall by walking the thumb across the joints, pinching each.
4. Wipe and reuse freely — the joints, not the images, are permanent.

citations.finding: Superior memorizers exploit ordered spatial scaffolds; familiar routes need no pre-learning — body maps are the most familiar of all
Gestural/motor encoding at study improves ordered recall vs. verbal-only encoding

evidence.caveats: Direct lab evidence is thin; support is by extension from loci + embodied-encoding literatures and long folk practice. Capacity is hard-capped at 12, and joints are less visually distinct than architectural loci.

use_when: ordered lists of ≤12 items (steps, a dozen, a small taxonomy)
field encoding with no pen, screen, or quiet room available
avoid_when: lists >12 (use a route palace)
items needing visually rich, sharply distinct loci — joints blur together
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Joint confusion: adjacent joints are too similar, images migrate.
- Traversal-direction drift: reversing the walk corrupts the order.

composes_with.note: a body palace with a fixed, pre-learned route
the placed image does the binding; the joint only orders it

worked_example.text: The 12 Earthly Branches cycle laid across the 12 finger joints — the classical 掐指一算 map; a modern equivalent: the OSI model's 7 layers pinched onto the first 7 joints in stack order.

batch_1_encoding.name: No-Repeat Imagery Rule
batch_1_encoding.definition: Within one palace, verse, or peg set, every anchor image must be unique — no repeated shapes, characters, or dominant colors — enforced as a hard authoring constraint.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: Distinctiveness is the engine of cue-based retrieval: duplicate images create cue overload (one cue → two targets) and fan-effect interference. The 千字文 (Thousand Character Classic, ~6th c.) was composed from exactly 1,000 non-repeating characters — uniqueness as a compositional law, millennia before the lab named it.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Keep a running inventory of images already used in the set.
2. Before placing a new image, diff it against the inventory on shape, dominant color, and action.
3. If two images collide, mutate the new one on at least one axis (scale, material, species, era).
4. Audit the finished set as a gallery walk — any two you'd confuse at a glance get regenerated.

citations.finding: Distinctive items in a homogeneous series are recalled 2–3x better — and distinctiveness collapses as soon as oddities repeat
Fan effect: each additional fact tied to one cue slows and weakens retrieval of all of them

evidence.caveats: The constraint raises authoring cost; applied dogmatically it can also ban *useful* repetition (deliberate callbacks must mutate the quoted image, not clone it).

use_when: any multi-item image set: palaces, peg lists, verse imagery
auditing an existing palace for retrieval failures
avoid_when: single standalone images (nothing to collide with)
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Near-duplicates slip in (two fires, two giants, two breaking objects) and cross-fire at recall.
- Over-correction: images mutated so far the fact mapping is lost.

composes_with.note: bizarreness only works if each bizarre item stays unique
palace images are the classic collision site

worked_example.text: A palace for HTTP methods already has 'a giant stamping hand' for PUT; POST cannot be another stamping hand — mutate it to a postal worker hurling a parcel through a mail slot.

batch_1_encoding.name: Etymology Decomposition
batch_1_encoding.definition: Decompose an abstract term into its concrete historical components (roots, radicals, affixes), then bind the meaning by composing the components into one story image.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: The term's opaque label is unpacked into 2–4 already-meaningful parts, so the label itself becomes a retrieval cue for the meaning (elaborative encoding + a built-in keyword structure). 说文解字 (~100 CE) analyzed characters into radicals + phonetics and glossed each with a compositional story — the same move as the Keyword Method, but the 'keyword' is the term's own components instead of a soundalike.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Split the term into its components (Greek/Latin roots, morphemes, or character radicals); verify the split, don't invent folk etymology.
2. Give each component a concrete stand-in image.
3. Compose ONE bizarre scene where the components interact to act out the modern meaning.
4. Recall path: term → components → scene → meaning. Rebuild it before every use until fluent.

citations.finding: Keyword-type decompositions robustly beat rote for vocabulary — etymology decomposition is the keyword method with semantic instead of acoustic links
Morphological instruction (teaching words via their roots/affixes) improved vocabulary and reading across grades

evidence.caveats: Etymology is not meaning: components cue the sense, they don't define it (folk etymologies mislead). Decomposition costs research time; skip it when the term is already transparent.

use_when: opaque technical or scientific terms (crypto-, -ectomy, ortho-)
jargon whose roots recur across a field — one decomposition pays off on many terms
avoid_when: terms whose historical components no longer relate to the current meaning
when a soundalike keyword is faster and equally imageable
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Invented etymology: a plausible-but-false split encodes a false fact.
- Decomposition without composition: parts listed, no scene, nothing sticks.

composes_with.note: same term→image→meaning path, semantic links instead of acoustic
the component scene is an interactive image
attach the decomposed term to a worked case

worked_example.text: Cryptography = kryptós ('hidden') + gráphein ('to write') → a hooded scribe writing with invisible ink under a cloak. Term → hidden + writing → the cloaked scribe → 'secret writing'.

batch_1_encoding.name: Master Exemplar
aliases: 永字八法 (Eight Principles of Yong)
batch_1_encoding.definition: Teach an entire skill category through ONE carefully chosen exemplar that contains every case of the category, mastered to fluency before any variant is introduced.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: A single artifact that embeds all the category's cases gives the learner one rich, integrated memory trace instead of N disconnected ones — each later variant is encoded as a delta against the exemplar, not a new item. 永字八法: the character 永 ('eternity') contains all eight fundamental brush strokes of Chinese calligraphy, so one character carries the whole stroke curriculum.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Survey the category and enumerate its cases (strokes, moves, patterns).
2. Choose (or construct) the one exemplar that contains every case; if none exists, take the minimal pair that covers them.
3. Drill the exemplar to fluency, naming each case as it appears.
4. Introduce variants as explicit diffs from the exemplar ('same as 永's dot, but pressed').

citations.finding: Worked examples outperform problem-solving for novices — one well-structured exemplar carries a whole procedure
Schema abstraction from analogs: variants are learned as mappings against a known source case

evidence.caveats: The exemplar must genuinely contain every case — a partial exemplar teaches a partial category with false confidence. Fluency on one exemplar does not guarantee transfer; variants must still be practiced.

use_when: procedures and skill categories (strokes, patterns, moves)
worked-example teaching where one artifact can embed every case
avoid_when: categories with no common structure (a forced exemplar distorts)
declarative facts — use imagery/verse instead
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Exemplar gaps: a case the exemplar lacks is never learned.
- Exemplar worship: the learner reproduces the exemplar but cannot diff variants against it.

composes_with.note: one maximally-informative worked example
variants taught as deltas are concrete examples
mix variants after the exemplar is fluent

worked_example.text: 永字八法: the side dot, horizontal, vertical, hook, rising, left-falling, and right-falling strokes all live in 永. Master the one character; every other character is a recombination.

batch_1_encoding.name: Two-Part Allegorical Encoding
batch_1_encoding.definition: Encode a meaning as a two-part saying: a vivid concrete mini-scene first, then the punchline that states the meaning — the scene is the retrieval cue, the punchline the fact.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: A concrete scene does the encoding work and the punchline does the decoding, so recall runs scene → meaning without rote pairing (elaborative imagery plus a self-contained hint). 歇后语 institutionalizes it: 'a mud bodhisattva crosses the river — it can't even save itself' (泥菩萨过河 — 自身难保); the image guarantees the meaning. It is the folk version of the metaphorAnchor pattern: a concrete scene that points without needing the answer stated first.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Take the abstract fact and find a concrete scene whose PHYSICS enact it (dissolving mud = helplessness).
2. Compress the scene to one clause — pictureable in under a second.
3. Attach the punchline: the plain meaning, stated after a dash.
4. Drill recall scene-first: hear the scene, produce the meaning; then reverse.

citations.finding: Imagery with the meaning supplied (vs. discovered or absent) produces the strongest paired-associate recall
Concreteness effect: imageable phrases recalled far better than abstract ones

evidence.caveats: Mostly tradition + mechanism-extension evidence; few controlled studies on xiehouyu per se. Culture-bound scenes don't transfer; the scene must enact the meaning, not merely co-occur with it.

use_when: abstract principles, warnings, and characterizations that need a portable one-liner
meanings a learner must generate from a hint (pre-reveal cue design)
avoid_when: precision-critical facts (the punchline paraphrases)
cross-cultural audiences who don't share the scene's conventions
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Scene recalled, punchline forgotten — the two halves were never drilled as a pair.
- Decorative scene: vivid but not enacting the meaning, so it cues nothing.

composes_with.note: the scene half is an interactive image
scene (visual) + punchline (verbal) is dual coding in one saying
the scene is a miniature concrete example

worked_example.text: 泥菩萨过河 — 自身难保: 'a mud bodhisattva crosses the river — it can't even save itself'. The dissolving clay IS the meaning; you cannot hold the image without the helplessness.

batch_1_encoding.name: Acrostic Verse
batch_1_encoding.definition: Compose a verse whose line-initial characters (or words) spell out, in order, the hidden key term or ordered list to be memorized — the verse is the carrier, the initials are the payload.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: The verse's meter and meaning hold the lines in order, and each line's first character is the retrieval cue for one item — a double scaffold (narrative order + orthographic cue). 藏头诗 is the classical Chinese form, predating the Western first-letter mnemonic (ROYGBIV-type acrostics) by roughly a millennium.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Write the key term or ordered list vertically; each item becomes one line's initial.
2. Draft lines that both fit the meter and make the initial natural — never force a wrong initial for smoothness.
3. Prefer lines whose content also relates to the item (double coding).
4. Recall by reading down the initials; drill list → verse and verse → list.

citations.finding: Mnemonic devices as organizers: first-letter cues reliably support ordered serial recall
Organizational factors in memory: imposed structure (including acronym/acrostic structure) improves list recall

evidence.caveats: Works for the FIRST item per line only — mid-line content isn't cued. Composition cost is high; a forced acrostic produces verse so strained it competes with the payload.

use_when: a key term or short ordered list that must come back verbatim and in order
ordered lists where a narrative carrier helps (the items alone are arid)
avoid_when: long lists (verse balloons)
lists whose items share initial characters — the cue stops discriminating
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Initials recalled, items not: the cue → item link was never drilled.
- Strained verse: the carrier is so unnatural it is forgotten before the payload.

composes_with.note: a verse whose initials carry the payload
the line initials act as ordered pegs

worked_example.text: Encoding the OSI layers bottom-up with an English acrostic: 'People Do Need To See Packets Arrive' (Physical, Data-link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application) — the 藏头诗 move with first letters as the payload.

batch_1_encoding.name: Shape Rhyme
batch_1_encoding.definition: A short verse that verbally describes the VISUAL SHAPE of the thing memorized (a diagram, symbol, structure, or notation), so reciting the verse redraws the picture in the mind.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: The verse runs in the phonological loop while its content reconstructs the image — the two codes cue each other, a self-contained dual-coding loop. The 八卦 trigram rhymes are the canonical form: 乾三连、坤六断、震仰盂、艮覆碗… ('Qián: three unbroken; Kūn: six broken; Zhèn: an upturned bowl; Gèn: an overturned bowl') — eight lines that verbally redraw all eight trigram figures.
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Look at the figure and name its salient shape features as concrete objects (bowls, breaks, stacks).
2. Fix a meter and make each line depict one element, in reading order.
3. Recite while tracing/drawing the figure until the verse alone suffices.
4. Drill both directions: verse → sketch, and sketch → verse.

citations.finding: Dual coding: verbal and imaginal traces with referential links give two retrieval routes
Working memory model: the phonological loop can maintain and replay verbal descriptions that reconstruct visuospatial content

evidence.caveats: Only works when the shape is simple enough to verbalize losslessly; complex diagrams need a real diagram. The verse describes appearance, not meaning — pair it with the concept separately.

use_when: diagrams, symbols, structures, and notation that must be redrawn from memory
small fixed visual sets (trigrams, circuit symbols, notation glyphs)
avoid_when: figures too detailed for a line-per-feature verse
when the MEANING rather than the shape is the target
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- Verse fluent, figure wrong: the descriptions were too vague to constrain the drawing.
- Shape without sense: the learner redraws the figure but can't say what it means.

composes_with.note: phonological loop + visuospatial sketchpad in one artifact
verse whose content is depictive rather than narrative
ask WHY the figure has that shape to attach meaning

worked_example.text: 乾三连、坤六断、震仰盂、艮覆碗、离中虚、坎中满、兑上缺、巽下断 — each line verbally redraws one trigram; recite all eight and the full 八卦 figure set is reconstructed.

batch_1_encoding.name: Antithetical Parallel Pairing
aliases: 笠翁对韵 Style (Lǐ Wēng's Rhyme Pairs)
batch_1_encoding.definition: Learn contrasts, opposites, and complementary pairs by drilling them in strict parallel form — matched length, matched structure, antithetical content — so each half cues the other.
batch_1_encoding.mechanism: The parallel syntax is a relational scaffold: once the structure is fixed, recalling one member reinstates the slot its partner must fill, and the antithesis supplies the semantic direction (relational structure as retrieval cue). 对仗 and the primer 笠翁对韵 drill it at scale: 天对地，雨对风，大陆对长空 ('sky pairs earth, rain pairs wind, broad land pairs the long sky').
batch_1_encoding.how_to:
1. Identify the pairs in the material (opposites, complements, cause/effect).
2. Phrase each pair in matched structure — same length, same grammar, antithetical content.
3. Chain the pairs rhythmically and drill them as call-and-response.
4. Test one direction only (given A, produce B), then reverse.

citations.finding: Associative symmetry and structure: organized pairs are recalled better than unorganized ones
Encoding specificity: a cue reinstates its encoding context — the parallel slot reinstates the partner

evidence.caveats: Suited only to genuinely pairable content; forcing unpairable material into antithesis creates false symmetries. Tradition evidence is strong (centuries of primer use), controlled evidence is by mechanism extension.

use_when: contrasts, opposites, and complementary pairs (synonyms/antonyms, dual concepts, trade-offs)
taxonomies that decompose into binary distinctions
avoid_when: unpairable or many-member sets (use chunking or a palace)
when the relationship between members is not actually antithetical
batch_1_encoding.failure_modes:
- False symmetry: a forced parallel implies a contrast that doesn't exist.
- Half-learned pairs: A cues B but B doesn't cue A (drilled one direction only).

composes_with.note: structure, not imagery, is the second code
each pair is a two-item relational chunk
asking why the pair contrasts deepens the link

worked_example.text: 天对地，雨对风，大陆对长空 — 'sky/earth, rain/wind, broad land/long sky': each strictly parallel pair is self-cueing; hear the first member and the slot demands its opposite.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Retrieval Practice
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Deliberately recalling target material from memory (self-testing, free recall, flashcards) instead of restudying it.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Effortful successful retrieval modifies the trace itself: it strengthens and diversifies retrieval routes, re-encodes the item in the current context, and elaborates cue–target links — effects restudy cannot produce. Retrieval attempts also potentiate encoding of subsequent feedback.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. After initial exposure, close the book and recall — free recall or short-answer beats recognition.
2. Check and give corrective feedback, especially on errors and omissions.
3. Repeat on a spaced schedule; keep difficulty high enough to require effort but allow success.
4. Test at the level of the target skill (derivations, not definitions, if derivations are the goal).

citations.finding: Tested group beat rereaders at 1 week despite losing at 5 minutes — the fluency illusion in one figure
Meta-analysis: g ≈ 0.50 across 159 effects; larger with feedback and effortful formats
Meta-analysis: g ≈ 0.61, robust across ages, formats, and settings

evidence.caveats: Feedback is required when success rates are low. Size of benefit for very complex, highly interlinked material is still debated (element interactivity).

use_when: any fact or concept after initial exposure — the tutor's default verb
pre-exam consolidation
diagnosing what a learner actually knows
avoid_when: zero prior exposure without feedback (use pretesting deliberately or worked examples instead)
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Recognition-only quizzing (multiple choice with weak distractors) breeds familiarity illusions.
- Tests so easy they require no retrieval effort, or so hard nothing is retrieved and no feedback follows.

composes_with.note: spaced retrieval is the core SRS loop — the two biggest effects compound
relearning-to-criterion is retrieval practice with a mastery rule
test outcomes are the ground truth that corrects self-judgment

worked_example.text: After teaching subnetting, don't re-display the table. Ask: 'How many usable hosts in a /26?' and require the derivation (2^(32−26) − 2 = 62). The derivation is the retrieval target, so the derivation is what gets tested.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Spaced / Distributed Practice
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Distribute study and retrieval episodes over increasing time gaps rather than massing them into one session.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Three converging accounts: encoding variability (each session stores fresh contextual cues), study-phase retrieval (reviewing a *partly forgotten* trace forces harder, more potent reprocessing), and between-session consolidation. Forgetting between reps is the feature, not the bug.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Schedule each review near the edge of forgetting — effortful but recoverable.
2. Expand gaps after successes; contract after failures.
3. Scale gaps to the retention goal (longer horizon → longer gaps, roughly 10–20% of the retention interval).
4. Automate per-item scheduling (SM-2/FSRS-style) keyed to recall outcomes.

citations.finding: Meta-analysis of 839 assessments: spaced beat massed in ~96% of comparisons
Optimal gap scales with retention interval (~10–20% of RI)
Rated one of only two high-utility techniques across learners and materials

evidence.caveats: Optimal-gap prescriptions are coarse; expanding vs. equal-interval differences are small. The big win is any spacing at all versus massing.

use_when: everything intended to last beyond a week
scheduling layer for all other techniques
avoid_when: genuinely one-shot needs with no retention goal (then cramming is rationally fine)
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Gaps so long that retrieval fails without feedback — relearning cost spikes.
- Massing disguised as spacing: many 'sessions' within one sitting.

composes_with.note: space the *tests*, not just the exposures
mixing topics automatically spaces each topic
criterion relearning across spaced sessions

worked_example.text: A fact first recalled correctly today gets reviewed at ~1d → 3d → 7d → 16d → 5w, each successful recall stretching the next gap; a failure resets to a short gap with feedback. That policy *is* the spacing literature operationalized.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Interleaving
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Mix problem types or categories within a session (ABCABC) instead of blocking them (AAABBB).
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Forces discriminative contrast: the learner must repeatedly decide *which* concept or method applies, not just execute it — blocking lets them ride the surface cue 'same as the last one'. Also embeds spacing between same-type items for free.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Identify confusable categories (sibling concepts, similar problem types).
2. Shuffle practice items across them within a session.
3. Strip surface cues that give the category away (e.g., chapter headers).
4. Warn the learner it will *feel* worse while working better.

citations.finding: Interleaved math practice hurt practice-session accuracy but roughly doubled test performance
Interleaving painters' works improved style induction; learners still judged blocking better — a metacognitive illusion
Meta-analysis g ≈ 0.42; strongest for confusable categories, weak or negative for unrelated material

evidence.caveats: Only pays when categories are genuinely confusable; interleaving unrelated topics is just noise. Brief initial blocking can help absolute novices before mixing.

use_when: confusable sibling nodes (sorting algorithms, cipher modes, verb conjugations)
when learners pattern-match instead of choosing methods
avoid_when: mutually unrelated topics
the very first exposures to a brand-new category
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Learner demotivation from lower practice accuracy, without framing.
- 'Interleaving' across unrelated domains that share no discrimination problem.

composes_with.note: mixing implies within-type spacing
interleaved quizzing is both at once
contrasting cases side by side drive schema discrimination

worked_example.text: Drill AES modes with shuffled prompts — an ECB question, then GCM, then CBC — so the learner must *select* the relevant property (deterministic leakage vs. authenticated encryption vs. IV misuse) rather than pattern-matching within a blocked ECB section.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Successive Relearning
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: In each of several spaced sessions, practice retrieval with feedback until every item is recalled correctly (criterion), then relearn to criterion again in the next session.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Welds the two largest effects — retrieval practice and spacing — with a mastery rule that guarantees each session ends in successful retrieval and automatically allocates more trials to weaker items.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Define the item set and a criterion (typically 1 correct recall per session; ~3 in the first).
2. Quiz with feedback; misses re-enter the within-session queue until correct.
3. Repeat across ≥3 sessions spaced days apart.
4. Retire items after stable criterion performance; resurrect on later failure.

citations.finding: ~3 initial correct recalls plus spaced relearning sessions gave the best durability-per-trial trade-off
Classroom deployment improved exam performance on targeted concepts by roughly a letter grade

evidence.caveats: Fewer independent labs than its parent effects (which are rock-solid); criterion bookkeeping needs tooling — a non-issue for a software tutor.

use_when: core facts, definitions, and mappings that must be durable
the default policy for an SRS-backed tutor's 'known node' maintenance
avoid_when: goals demanding transfer beyond recall — add elaboration and varied problems on top
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Criterion met via recognition or ordering cues rather than genuine recall.
- Sessions spaced too tightly — criterion becomes trivial and the spacing benefit evaporates.

composes_with.note: retrieval with a mastery rule
the sessions are the spacing
measured criterion supersedes flawed self-judgment of 'knowing it'

worked_example.text: Tutor policy: every key KG node must be recalled correctly once per session across ≥3 sessions ≥2 days apart; a miss re-queues the node later in the same session until recalled, and postpones retirement.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Elaboration / Elaborative Interrogation
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: For each to-be-learned statement, generate an answer to 'Why is this true?' / 'Why does this make sense?'
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Forces integration with prior knowledge: the generated explanation becomes a retrieval structure and discriminates the fact from similar facts. The *generation* is the active ingredient — supplied explanations do far less.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Present the fact.
2. Prompt: 'Why would this be true?' and require a produced answer.
3. Verify or correct the explanation (a tutor must catch confabulated 'whys').
4. Reuse the explanation as a retrieval cue in later testing.

citations.finding: Answering why-questions roughly doubled fact learning vs. reading
Rated moderate utility: reliable for factual material, thinner evidence for complex content and low-knowledge learners

evidence.caveats: Depends on adequate prior knowledge to generate correct explanations; wrong self-generated 'whys' encode errors unless checked.

use_when: discrete facts with underlying causes
learners with relevant background
Socratic follow-ups after a correct recall
avoid_when: true rote arbitrariness (there is no why to port 443)
novices with nothing to connect to
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Confabulated explanations encoded as fact.
- Shallow restatement ('because it is') passing as elaboration.

composes_with.note: recall the fact, then interrogate it
EI targets facts; self-explanation targets steps and texts

worked_example.text: Fact: 'TLS 1.3 removed static RSA key exchange.' Why? → Because without ephemeral keys, a later server-key compromise retroactively decrypts recorded traffic; mandating (EC)DHE buys forward secrecy. The generated *why* is the glue that makes the fact stick and transfer.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Self-Explanation
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: While studying a worked example or text, the learner explains each step to themselves — what it does, why it's justified, and how it connects to principles.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Gap detection and repair: articulating each step exposes missing inferences, forces their generation, and integrates the material with existing schemas. The benefit concentrates in *generated* inferences, not paraphrase.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Work through material step by step.
2. At each step ask: what happened, why is it legal/justified, what principle licenses it?
3. Flag any step you can only describe, not justify — that's a gap.
4. Resolve gaps against the source, then re-explain without it.

citations.finding: Successful physics learners spontaneously self-explained worked examples; weak learners paraphrased
Prompted self-explanation improved learning from text, especially deep questions
Meta-analysis g ≈ 0.55 across 69 studies; prompt design moderates strongly

evidence.caveats: Time-expensive; paraphrasing masquerades as explaining; providing explanations instead of eliciting them forfeits most of the benefit.

use_when: worked examples, proofs, code reading
any stepwise procedure with hidden rationale
avoid_when: time-critical drilling of already-understood material
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Paraphrase loops (restating without inference).
- Explaining to the tutor's satisfaction rather than to coherence — gaming the prompt.

composes_with.note: the canonical pairing: study example + explain each step
Feynman is self-explanation aimed at an imagined novice

worked_example.text: Reading concurrency code line by line: 'Why does this mutex guard *this* field and not that one? What invariant holds between lock and unlock?' The moment a line can be described but not justified, a gap has been located and can be repaired.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Feynman Technique
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Explain a concept from scratch in plain language as if teaching a novice; locate the point where you stall; return to the source; simplify and re-explain.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: A composite protocol: closed-book explanation (retrieval practice) + step justification (self-explanation) + a built-in calibration probe (the stall point is a *measured* gap) + benefits of teaching expectancy and audience-tuned reorganization.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Closed book, write/say the explanation for an imagined novice.
2. Mark every stall, hedge, or jargon-hiding-a-gap.
3. Return to sources for exactly those points.
4. Re-explain simpler; iterate until it flows without props.

citations.finding: Actually teaching (and preparing to teach) improved comprehension and transfer vs. studying for a test
Mere expectation of teaching improved recall and organization of studied material

evidence.caveats: The branded technique itself has no controlled trials; its ingredients are evidenced separately. Treat as a packaging of retrieval + self-explanation + calibration, not a distinct mechanism.

use_when: conceptual material after initial study
audit of supposed understanding before an exam or design review
avoid_when: pure fact sets with no explanatory structure
as a first exposure method
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Fluent analogy that hides the gap instead of exposing it.
- Skipping the loop-back step — the stall must trigger source revisits.

composes_with.note: audience framing adds simplification pressure
closed-book explanation is free recall
stall points are objective gap measurements

worked_example.text: Explain public-key crypto with the open-padlock analogy until the stall arrives: 'wait — if anyone can lock, how does *signing* work? That's the padlock backwards...' The stall pinpoints exactly which half of the keypair asymmetry was never actually understood.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Concrete Examples
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Illustrate an abstract principle with multiple concrete, varied examples, then fade toward the abstract representation as understanding grows.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Abstract rules are hard to encode and retrieve cold; concrete cases give imageable, relatable hooks. Comparing *several varied* examples is the active ingredient — it lets the learner abstract the shared deep structure while discarding surface features. Concreteness fading transfers the grounding to a portable symbolic form.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. State the principle, then give 2–3 examples that differ on the surface but share the deep structure.
2. Prompt the learner to identify what's common across them (the principle) and what's incidental.
3. Fade: move from richly concrete to schematic to symbolic once the pattern is seen.
4. Have the learner generate a fresh example as a transfer check.

citations.finding: Concreteness fading (concrete → idealized) beat purely concrete or purely abstract training on transfer
Review: fading from concrete to abstract supports transfer better than either alone across math learning
Practitioner review: varied concrete examples aid comprehension and transfer of abstract ideas

evidence.caveats: A single example (or examples too alike) breeds surface-bound, non-transferring knowledge. Highly concrete/seductive examples can also anchor the learner to irrelevant details.

use_when: abstract or formal principles
definitions that learners can state but not apply
before asking for transfer to novel cases
avoid_when: material that is already concrete
when a single vivid example would over-anchor (need varied set instead)
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- One example → the learner learns the example, not the principle.
- Surface-similar examples → no discrimination of deep vs. incidental features.
- Never fading → the concept stays welded to its scaffold.

composes_with.note: diagram the example, not just describe it
contrasting cases across categories drives schema discrimination
a worked example is a concrete example carried through a full solution

worked_example.text: Teaching 'confused deputy': show SSRF (a server tricked into fetching an internal URL), then CSRF (a browser tricked into sending an authenticated request), then a classic compiler-billing example. Same deep structure — an authorized agent misused by a third party — surfaced across three concrete skins, then named as the abstract principle.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Worked Examples
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Give novices fully solved problems to study (each step shown and justified) rather than problems to solve unaided, then fade steps toward independent problem-solving as competence grows.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: For novices, unguided problem-solving spends working memory on means-ends search and leaves none for schema construction. A worked example removes the search, freeing capacity to encode the solution *pattern*. Completion/faded examples gradually reintroduce the effort as schemas form.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Present a complete, step-by-step solution with each step's rationale.
2. Have the learner self-explain each step (the pairing that makes worked examples work).
3. Fade: blank out the last step, then more steps (completion problems), until the learner solves unaided.
4. Switch to retrieval practice / independent problems once competence is established.

citations.finding: Studying worked examples produced faster, better algebra learning than solving equivalent problems for novices
Gradually fading worked steps into problem-solving improved learning and near/far transfer
Expertise reversal: worked examples help novices but hurt more advanced learners, for whom solving is better

evidence.caveats: Expertise reversal is the governing rule — the technique's benefit shrinks and then reverses as the learner gains competence; the tutor must hand off to retrieval practice as mastery rises.

use_when: novices meeting a new problem type
multi-step procedures with high element interactivity
before independent problem-solving
avoid_when: learners who already have the schema (expertise reversal — switch to solving)
single-step trivial problems
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Passive example-watching without self-explanation → shallow copying.
- Keeping learners on worked examples past competence → wasted effort and boredom (expertise reversal).

composes_with.note: worked examples only pay when each step is self-explained
studied solutions become retrievable solution-schema chunks
fade into independent solving as competence grows

worked_example.text: Teaching a buffer-overflow exploit: first walk the complete solved chain (find offset → control EIP → locate a JMP ESP → place shellcode), justifying each step; then fade — the learner supplies the offset, then the gadget, then the whole chain — before handing them a fresh binary to own unaided.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Generation Effect
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Information that the learner generates (produces, completes, or fills in) is remembered better than the same information read passively.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Producing an answer engages the target's semantic and cue–target relations more deeply than reading it, and commits the learner to a response that feedback can then correct. Overlaps with retrieval practice; the distinguishing case is generation with partial cues rather than pure recall.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Replace 'read this' with 'produce this': cloze deletions, fill-in-the-blank, predict-the-output, complete-the-partial-solution.
2. Keep the generation within reach (partial cues) so it succeeds with effort.
3. Always give immediate feedback — generated errors stick too.
4. Escalate from heavily cued generation toward free recall.

citations.finding: Words generated from a rule + cue were recalled better than read words — the original generation effect
Meta-analysis: reliable generation advantage, g ≈ 0.40, moderated by difficulty and feedback

evidence.caveats: Generated *errors* without feedback are encoded and persist. The benefit narrows when generation is too hard (nothing produced) or trivially easy. Cloze deletion in an SRS is applied generation.

use_when: converting passive material into active production
cloze/flashcard authoring
predict-then-verify exercises
avoid_when: no feedback loop available
content the learner cannot yet generate at all (use worked examples first)
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Generation without feedback cements wrong answers.
- 'Generation' so cued it is really reading (no productive effort).

composes_with.note: free-recall retrieval is maximal generation; cloze is partial-cue generation
generated cloze items ride the SRS schedule
guessing before instruction is generation under uncertainty

worked_example.text: Instead of showing `chmod 750 file` and its meaning, prompt: 'Owner rwx, group r-x, others none → what octal?' The learner generates 750 (and can verify), encoding the permission-bit mapping far better than reading the mapping table.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Pretesting
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Have learners attempt to answer questions on material *before* studying it, even though they will mostly fail, then teach the answers.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: The failed retrieval attempt primes the memory system: it activates related knowledge, exposes the gap, and makes the subsequent correct answer more salient and better encoded when feedback arrives. Curiosity and attention to the answer both rise after a miss.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Pose target questions before instruction; require a genuine attempt (a guess, not a shrug).
2. Immediately follow with the correct answer / the lesson.
3. Keep pretests focused on what the lesson will actually cover.
4. Frame errors as expected and useful, to protect motivation.

citations.finding: Answering prequestions (and failing) before reading improved later test performance on that material vs. extra study time
Unsuccessful retrieval attempts before study enhanced later learning of the answers

evidence.caveats: Requires prompt feedback (an unanswered pretest just frustrates). Benefit concentrates on pretested and closely related content; may not spread to untested material. Motivation cost if failure isn't framed.

use_when: opening move of a lesson
activating prior knowledge before new material
a Socratic tutor's first question on a topic
avoid_when: no feedback will follow
high-anxiety learners without careful error framing
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Pretest without a prompt answer → wasted frustration.
- Testing content the lesson won't address → no priming payoff.

composes_with.note: pretesting is generation under uncertainty before instruction
pretest opens, spaced retrieval consolidates
a failed 'why' pretest primes the explanation that follows

worked_example.text: Before teaching CSRF defenses, ask: 'How would you stop a forged cross-site request?' The learner guesses ('check the referer?') and mostly misses — then when the lesson introduces same-site cookies and CSRF tokens, those answers land against a primed, gap-aware slot.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Metacognition & Calibration
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Monitoring what you do and don't know accurately, and using that judgment to allocate study — with an emphasis on correcting the systematic illusions that make monitoring wrong.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Learners judge learning from fluency cues (ease of reading, familiarity) that dissociate from actual retrievability, producing overconfidence. Delayed and test-based judgments realign the judgment with reality; accurate monitoring then drives efficient restudy allocation.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Elicit a confidence prediction before checking (predict recall, then test).
2. Compare prediction to outcome; score the miscalibration (e.g., Brier score).
3. Prefer *delayed* judgments and actual retrieval over immediate 'feels known' ratings.
4. Route study time to overconfident items — the ones judged known but failed.

citations.finding: Delayed judgments of learning were far more accurate than immediate ones (the delayed-JOL effect)
Foresight bias: presence of the answer at study inflates predicted future recall — an illusion of competence

evidence.caveats: Monitoring accuracy is domain- and cue-dependent; simply asking for confidence doesn't fix it — the correction comes from delayed, test-based feedback that breaks the fluency illusion.

use_when: deciding what to restudy or reschedule
surfacing over/underconfidence to the learner
driving a confidence-weighted scheduler
avoid_when: as a substitute for actual testing (self-report alone is the weak part)
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Trusting immediate 'I know this' fluency (the core illusion).
- Confidence collected but never checked against outcomes → no calibration.

composes_with.note: test outcomes are the ground truth that recalibrates judgment
a measured criterion beats self-judged 'knowing it'
overconfident-but-failed items get scheduled sooner

worked_example.text: Before each drill the tutor asks the learner to predict recall (0–100%); it Brier-scores prediction vs. outcome and flags systematically overconfident nodes — feeding the KG's confidence weights so bluffed-but-failed topics resurface sooner. Calibration data *is* scheduler input.

batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.name: Desirable Difficulties
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.definition: Umbrella principle: conditions that make practice harder and slower in the moment (spacing, interleaving, testing, generation, varied practice) often produce more durable, transferable learning — while easy conditions inflate immediate performance without learning.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.mechanism: Current performance (how fluent you are now) and learning (durable change in retrievability) are dissociable. Difficulties that force effortful reconstruction — retrieval, spacing, interleaving, generation — increase learning even as they depress momentary performance; fluent, massed, re-read practice does the reverse.
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.how_to:
1. Recognize when performance is high but learning may be low (massing, rereading, cueing).
2. Deliberately inject the evidenced difficulties: space it, mix it, test it, make them generate it, vary the conditions.
3. Keep difficulty *desirable* — effortful yet surmountable with feedback, not defeating.
4. Warn learners that it will feel worse; use outcome data to prove it works.

citations.finding: Introduced 'desirable difficulties': manipulations that impair acquisition performance but enhance long-term retention and transfer
Review formalizing the learning-vs-performance distinction across spacing, interleaving, testing, and variability

evidence.caveats: 'Desirable' is load-bearing: a difficulty helps only if the learner has the resources to overcome it (feedback, prior knowledge). Undesirable difficulties (poor legibility, missing background) just hurt. Learners reliably misjudge which condition is better (they prefer the ineffective easy one).

use_when: choosing between an easy-feeling and an effortful practice design
explaining to learners why the harder path is chosen
as the meta-rationale behind spacing/interleaving/testing
