avoid_when: the learner lacks the prerequisites or feedback to overcome the difficulty (then it's just undesirable)
batch_2_retrieval_scheduling_metacogniti.failure_modes:
- Adding difficulty the learner can't overcome (no feedback, missing background) — undesirable, pure harm.
- Optimizing for practice-session performance (looks great, learns little).

composes_with.note: spacing is a canonical desirable difficulty
mixing is a desirable difficulty
effortful recall is a desirable difficulty
producing rather than reading is a desirable difficulty

worked_example.text: Re-reading the exploit walkthrough five times feels productive (high fluency) but teaches little; making the learner reconstruct the chain from memory, days apart, mixed with other CVEs, feels worse and harder — and that discomfort is the signature of the conditions that actually build durable skill.

taxonomy.description: How the techniques relate by function. Encoding gets material in; retrieval and generative strategies strengthen and integrate it; scheduling decides when practice happens; metacognitive strategies decide what to practice and govern the rest.

top_five.why: Largest and most replicated learning effect; recalling beats restudying across ages, formats, and materials. It is the tutor's default verb — every 'known' node should be tested, not re-shown.
The highest-leverage scheduling decision; distributing practice multiplies the benefit of everything else and is the substrate of the SRS the tutor already runs.
A turnkey policy that operationalizes retrieval + spacing with a mastery criterion — the cleanest default for maintaining durable knowledge of core KG nodes.

The evidence-backed on-ramp for novices; studying faded solutions builds schemas without overloading working memory, and the expertise-reversal rule tells the tutor exactly when to hand off to retrieval practice.
Exploits the KG's structure directly: scheduling confusable sibling nodes together forces discrimination and yields transfer that blocked practice cannot.

myths.name: Learning Styles (matching modality to learner)
myths.claim: Teaching to a learner's preferred 'style' (visual/auditory/kinesthetic) improves learning.
myths.why_false: No credible evidence for the matching (meshing) hypothesis; controlled tests find matching instruction to stated style gives no benefit. Everyone benefits from dual coding regardless of 'style'.

myths.name: Rereading & Highlighting Are Effective Study
myths.claim: Rereading notes and highlighting text are good ways to learn.
myths.why_false: Both rated low-utility: they inflate fluency and familiarity without building durable retrieval strength. Retrieval practice and spacing dominate them.

myths.name: Photographic / Eidetic Memory
myths.claim: Some people have a literal photographic memory; champions are born with special brains.
myths.why_false: No verified adult eidetikers; memory champions show normal memory and normal brain structure, using trainable spatial mnemonics — not innate hardware.

myths.name: Learning Pyramid / Dale's Cone Percentages
myths.claim: We remember 10% of what we read, 20% of what we hear, 90% of what we teach, etc.
myths.why_false: The fixed retention percentages are fabricated — traceable to no study and often attached to invented citations. The numbers are pseudoscience even though 'teaching helps' is separately true.

myths.name: Brain-Training Games Improve General Cognition
myths.claim: Commercial brain-training transfers to broad real-world intelligence and memory.
myths.why_false: Consensus review finds gains are largely task-specific; little credible evidence of far transfer to everyday cognitive performance.

citation.authors: Simons, Boot, Charness, Gathercole, Chabris, Hambrick & Stine-Morrow

myths.name: The Mozart Effect
myths.claim: Listening to Mozart durably raises intelligence.
myths.why_false: Meta-analysis finds no reliable IQ benefit; early effects were tiny, transient arousal/mood effects, not lasting cognitive gains.
